selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a retired man who has always been careful about the information he consumes, even before social media became mainstream, I feel that dealing with fake content now is quite similar to what one might have experienced in print or broadcast media back in my day. Having spent most of my life reading newspapers and magazines, I've learned to be skeptical. It's not just about trusting sources but knowing who you are supposed to trust.\n\nMy own bachelor's degree helped me understand that authorities don't always agree on matters, so it taught me a bit about critical thinking without being too formal—like learning how to read between the lines in legal documents or scientific papers when I was studying. Even though my father never went to school and only had no formal education while my mother earned her PhD later in life, they both gave me different perspectives on knowledge: one through practical wisdom from a working-class background, the other through academic rigor.\n\nThe main thing for me is not having spent time online myself—I'm retired and don't use social media—but I know what it takes to spot misinformation. It's about knowing your own standards and sticking with them regardless of how good the presentation seems.",

My father had no formal education but was practical; this shaped my distrust in unverified claims.\n3. The fact that I don't use social media means I rely on what others say about it and established methods of verification.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a retired man who has always been careful about the information he consumes, even before social media became mainstream, I feel that dealing with fake content now is quite similar to what one might have experienced in print or broadcast media back in my day. Having spent most of my life reading newspapers and magazines, I've learned to be skeptical. It's not just about trusting sources but knowing who you are supposed to trust.

My own bachelor's degree helped me understand that authorities don't always agree on matters, so it taught me a bit about critical thinking without being too formal—like learning how to read between the lines in legal documents or scientific papers when I was studying. Even though my father never went to school and only had no formal education while my mother earned her PhD later in life, they both gave me different perspectives on knowledge: one through practical wisdom from a working-class background, the other through academic rigor.

The main thing for me is not having spent time online myself—I'm retired and don't use social media—but I know what it takes to spot misinformation. It's about knowing your own standards and sticking with them regardless of how good the presentation seems.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. As a person with high conscientiousness from my studies, I approach information critically.
2. My father had no formal education but was practical; this shaped my distrust in unverified claims.
3. The fact that I don't use social media means I rely on what others say about it and established methods of verification.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, measured, experienced with a touch of old-world skepticism.
